Media notes: Flagging for unique call, what I'll describe as a "whistle-chirp". Seen alone on ~10' perch in the Allen St sumac, but with other robins in earshot. Took a little while to track down the source of the call, as this call was interspersed with regular robin calls, so I initially dismissed the robin I was looking at as the source until I was about fifteen feet away and could watch it it vocalizing.  Didn't think to make a guess at sex, though light was gray enough it wouldn't have been a confident guess anyway.  Call is not yet included in the Peterson Field Guide to Bird Sounds: https://academy.allaboutbirds.org/peterson-field-guide-to-bird-sounds/?speciesCodeamerob&speciesAmerican%20Robin%20-%20Turdus%20migratorius

Media notes: Part 2: unique caroling song after the recorded 'hisselly' song in part 1, sung by the same male after moving away from the pair of robins. His posture was normal.
Observation Details: Along a power line right-of-way and rarely used road, I recorded an amazing male with unusual songs. In the first 'hisselly' recording, it was near two other robins when vocalizing and in a stooped posture. The only possible mimicry that I can tell is Brown Creeper-like notes at the start and the phrases from 2:04-2:09 secs, which sounds a bit like Least or Yellow-bellied Flycatcher. For the 2nd recording, it moved away from the other robins and continued singing, this time in a normal upright posture, with its own unique individual caroling song. What a bird!

Observation Details: I heard them singing in four different voices from several directions. There may have been more but four is all I could verify, even though it is possible there were repeats. Birds do fly from one spot to another and then sing from the new spot, which can make it seem like another bird is singing. They get quite a chorus going in this neighbourhood with all its tall trees.

Media notes: Behavior and other notes: Adult male American Robin giving calls. He then transitioned into hisselly calls when another AMRO came into the area. The other AMRO only gave a call or two. The focal male continued hisselly calls until the second AMRO moved away.  During the long string of hisselly calls, he repeated calls mostly twice, some three times and some only once. He then gave calls, songs, then calls. At 4:08 another AMRO flew over the focal male and gave some calls. The focal male changed his call slightly while the other AMRO was in his territory. Once the other AMRO moved away, the focal male changed call type again. He then sat on this perch for many minutes after he stopped vocalizing, then flew off. Recorder HPF on at 80 Hz, -12 dB/octave. Locality: Ithaca--Etna Rd (42.4909,-76.4443) persimmon thrushes of powerful pines and pendulous berries (aka: hundred AMRO eating buckthorn and honeysuckle fruits) (01/23/24) (1110 ft.)
Observation Details: 100-300 . Amazing AMRO flock! Could be hundreds. — Over 80 counted at one quick bin of flock early in checklist: at least 40 seen close each side of road at once counted in quick loose 10’s, but dozens are moving everywhere I look from my center spot on road, and more heard distant and movement of them in background trees too. Must be more than 80, and surely many more than. — Dozens and dozens moving and calling and singing in background too, besides ones closer along road and the dozens flying over road between sides. — At least 100 but I really think must be many more than that, 150-200 prob a better guess. — Flock doesn’t extend very far along road, but does extend n/s into next row of conifers beyond deciduous.   — — Eating black buckthorn berries of tall shrubberies, also many dozens up in white pines, saw some apparently foraging along bare deciduous tree branches, and dozen w juncos on asphalt road when I arrived. Caleb saw AMRO eating red honeysuckle berries. Note: snowing now, forecast to snow 1.5-2 inches today with possible freezing ice too, then rains. Roads clear of snow, but forest floor still with several inches at least from past ~ week.   — — We haven’t seen an AMRO flock this big since moving to Ithaca, except for dusk flights of ~ 150 this past summer, and haven’t seen any AMRO in ~ weeks, I’ve missed them!  — — note: Caleb joins 20 min. into my checklist  — — “Interesting there are so many given there are so few everywhere else. And there doesn’t seem to be anything remarkable about the habitat; I mean, there are berry trees, but there are also in other places.” — “ I appreciate robins so much more after spending winter in a place that doesn’t really have them..” — “Me too. That’s why I’m spending an hour here looking at them.” — “Maybe I shouldn’t have been bored by the lack of rare birds in the Willamette Valley, and just enjoyed the robins.” — “I’ve always enjoyed big robin flocks.” — “I’ve always enjoyed the really big flocks.”   — — note: the Tompkins co. High Count on ebird is from this same time of year: Jan. 20, 2022, over 5,273 robins heading for night roost on w. side of Cayuga Lake :  https://ebird.org/checklist/S101107915

Observation Details: very cool encounter today, at the place where the tent used to be (asphalt paved rectangular area at northwest of horse range, it's taken down now so it's just empty space) there were perhaps 20 robins in a pretty big flock foraging together on the road and the tent area. pretty strange initially since I would think that robins forage in the grass, and it didn't seem like there were any seeds or stuff spilled on the roads. anyways, the robins allowed pretty close approach, I got a LOT of photos of them just foraging or standing there upright. I walked up pretty close to them, and luckily throughout the entire 15 mins there there were no cars coming so I was able to observed them undisturbed. sometimes the starlings would come perch on the ground next to them but quickly walk off, they didn't seem to mind the starlings much however. like the starlings, they would suddenly flush onto the trees, but were definitely less coordinated than the starling flocks (since they only flock in winter as opposed to what I would think the whole year for starlings) and aren't really flocking birds like blackbirds to begin with. I believe what I'm witnessing is the winter flocking behavior in starlings? where they gather in larger flocks to forage, which you'll probably not see in spring or summer. I got nice photos of one male that let me come pretty close, saw some females nearby and I think there was also an individual with some leucistic pigmentation, or just a late molt.  also robins were very vocal today in general, many of them calling (one recorded at north of westwood) and many more calling around ampitheatre just before I arrived at the old tent site

Observation Details: very abundant and vocal this morning - one robin in particular was perched on a tree near the first carolina wren, at the very top, and making all types of calls as it stayed at that spot for at least 10 mins during my course of observation. it made all kinds of calls that sounded vibrating like wood thrush, can't really describe it but recorded a lot of audio. didn't realize that robins could make that kind of call so well, thought it was more of a thing of catharus thrushes! later it started singing a typical robin song, but with a twist at the end - instead of just ending its musical notes, it also added that vibrating and cool sound like catharus thrush, audio also included. note that there is a lot of background noise in the audio since it's a noisy environment in the city in general with ongoing construction (pretty early in the morning) nearby and other machines humming and buzzing. some robins also seen flying in the small dog park in the middle of the street south of hyatt house later, seems like robins are very common here! definitely not used to hearing them singing so often in the fall, back at gs campus.

Media notes: Adult robin performing a repeated seet call was recorded and shared here. I saw the robin vocalizing and was perched on a branch at the lower end of a dead pine tree and I wasn't too far from the bird...it seemed quite content perched with me close by and vocalizing. I did not observe any aerial predators near by. But I also heard 1-2 individuals performing this vocalization well after sundown about a week ago during a thunderstorm and not sure how the birds saw any aerial predators in the dark.
Observation Details: Heard 2-3 robins performing this high pitched whistle that I'm not sure I've ever heard from robins before. The species account in the Birds of the World says about robin calls, "A third note is a high pitched, thin, whining whistle or seet call, not unlike the call of the Cedar Waxwing (Bombycilla cedrorum; RS); this is given in response to aerial predators (179, see Behavior: Predation)."

Media notes: I stood no more than 20 feet from an adult AMRO perched in a tree who made this hissing sound, which the Merlin app did not recognize.  A female House Sparrow on a branch near the AMRO looked quizzically at the AMRO, but the AMRO returned the gaze with apparent unconcern.  The hissing could have been a danger warning to an immature who was on the ground 20 feet from me in the opposite direction.  The immature flew to a tree when I turned to walk in its direction. (I did not notice it until I started walking).  Also heard:  Gray Catbird, Great-crested Flycatcher, another American Robin (erking), House Sparrow, Red-bellied Woodpecker, barking dog, handing and foot noise of recordist, wind, general atmospheric noise. High-pass filter applied at 246 Hz, 20 Hz roll off.

Observation Details: Two adult males seemed to be sizing each other up this morning, though there was no aggression. One bird I have never seen before. He had a destinctive, rounded shape because his tail feathers had all been lost at some point and were just beginning to grow back in from their shafts (see photos). This male perched near the window for several minutes, calling in high-pitched notes. I got good recordings of him. The resident male was on the ground below him, puffing out his red chest feathers, possibly to warn him off. Then he flew up, and took over the bob-tailed male's perch.

Media notes: Seet call given by American Robin on ground. Supposedly an aerial predator alarm call, but no accipiters or falcons were detected nearby.
Observation Details: One bird on ground giving high "seet" calls around 8-9 kHz. Recording added. A quick Google search indicates that this may be an aerial predator alarm call , but there was no aerial predator around this bird (or at all during my observation period). The bird may have seen me as the predator. I did not immediately recognize the call, and walked towards the sound, at which point the bird left the ground and went into a nearby tree. All very strange, and apparently inconsistent with the study, which concluded that this call was given only in response to aerial predators and not terrestrial predators.

Observation Details: Estimate.  About two dozens of them were on one of the lawns when suddenly they darted up all together screeching and dove into a bush making a deafening noise.  I looked around but saw no cause of alarm (most other birds, except for some starlings that had joined the robins, were going about their business and the Blue Jays were quiet).  A good minute or so later, the peregrine falcon appeared in the sky - the robins must have had advance warning by their sentinels.  They sounded very agitated (recording) and didn't come out of their hiding place for a long time.

Background Species: Bombycilla cedrorum -- Cedar Waxwing
Observation Details: We counted several large groups that were perched and also several groups on the move. They were identified by their medium-large size, large-bellied shape with long tails, and their coloration, including dark heads and backs, rufous/red underparts and yellow bills. Their flight style of pulling wings in toward the body after several wingbeats helped to identify the large flocks of birds. They seemed to be moving from west to east ahead of dark gray storm clouds and stopping briefly in tall leafless trees, calling to each other. Some groups would perch for about ten minutes while other groups were more active, flying over us and beyond. More and more kept coming in and alighting on trees and rooftops all around. This is the first time we have seen this much activity in the area! Recording and photos attached

Observation Details: Spectacular to see, American Robins were everywhere.  When I arrived I watched hundreds and hundreds of them flying northeast, which I counted by 10s, 20s, and 50s.  In just over a half-mile of going south to the Sunflower Workstation, I counted close to 3,000 birds flying overhead in scattered but very large groups that were flying from juniper slopes on the west side of the Sunflower Valley to the juniper slopes on the east side of the valley, heading northeast.  South of the workstation, the numbers of birds in flight dropped, but I had occasional large flocks here and there.  On top of the birds in flight, American Robins were heard everywhere on the juniper slopes on both sides of the road and I could see birds constantly flying around.  At times I would think there'd be 10 or so birds calling, but when a Cooper's Hawk made an ambush at one point, there'd be 100-150 birds that would fly up.  Very hard to say how many there were when lots of the birds didn't fly up for more of an obvious count.  At times the robins were loud to the point it was challenging to hear other birds.  It was fun to observe.

The adult female called numerous times and then ran along the ground, hopped up to the bush, fed the young a worm, and then flew off.    Update: I checked again in mid afternoon on August 16 but did not see or hear any robins.  I also checked twice on August 18 but didn't find them then either.

Observation Details: • 4:19 a.m. - right outside the window the pre-dawn silence was initially broken by an unseen singer loudly and ceaselessly blaring the full standard "cheerilup cheerily" song complete with sproingy "hissely" note terminating almost every phrase   • 4:23 a.m. 1st recording below - then a much quieter song was delivered for less than 2 minutes that consisted of note shapes similar but not identical to this singer's recorded "hissely" notes that sometimes terminate each phrase of the full standard song, and is a good match for Pieplow's example of the "Complex Song" apparently given by males in close courtship   • 4:25 a.m. 2nd recording below - the quiet song was followed by a mid-volume variation on standard song that lacked the "hissely" note at the end of each phrase, but by the end of this recording the singer's volume had increased and he began delivering the full song with terminal "hissely" note  • 4:26 a.m. 3rd recording below - about a minute and a half after the quiet song was heard, the singer was back to loudly blaring the full standard "cheerilup cheerily" song with terminal "hissely" notes

Media notes: mid-volume standard song variation followed the quiet song and lacked "hissely" terminal note at end of each phrase for most of recording (skittering at :20-:21 is feet on gutter?)

Interesting bird. The recording starts with some sort of whisper song or practice song including when the robin transitions to singing a typical song. While I was recording, the robin was facing toward a building, where the walls met at a 90-degree angle inward, such that the robin was able to leverage the structure of the building to reverberate and amplify its song. It sang both the atypical and typical song types facing into the walls in this way, receiving the benefit of the echo. The robin was alone, standing in a small flowering tree, and it switched from the atypical to the typical song when another robin flew by very close. Though it transitions songs, it stayed still. The timing of the fly-by and the transition was so simultaneous that it was my impression that the flyby initiated the switch.

Media notes: This American Robin appears to be using mimicry in its song (which is not unheard of, but I haven't heard it personally and have not recorded anything like this until now).  I have more audio of this same bird (which I may upload later); all of its songs are like this.  Mimicry or not, this doesn't sound like a typical song for the species.  In the first two song phrases, the two opening notes sound like a Baltimore Oriole; the final ones like a Northern Cardinal.  If the oriole comparison is stretching things, it is hard to imagine that the phrases at 1:03 and 1:42 aren't Northern Cardinal mimicry.  They sound remarkably accurate.  (There is no question about the bird's identity; I had it in view while recording it).  Normalized to -3 dB except for voice announcement at -10 dB.  24-bit/48 kHz WAV file edited with Ocenaudio. Observation Details: Robins were streaming north up the main road in the park 674 at dawn. My son and I stood just southwest of the visitor/registration center on the Long Way Home trail. We watched robins and waxwings stream north up the canyon. The robins were flying very low, from 15 to >50 feet above the ground. In most cases, the robins were streaming just above the pinyons and junipers, sometimes seeming to almost collide into us.  We could see robins streaming by at the bottom of the valley all the way up to the top of the western ridge as well as the eastern ridge.  The bulk of the passage was between 710-730 am, although robins continued to stream by after sunrise. At several points in time between 710-730 am, I looked southward and counted the number of robins passing by within my immediate field of view (~100 ft). On average, 40 birds per 10 seconds would pass through my field of view, amounting to ~4,800 passing through within 20 minutes. Extrapolating across the valley, resulted in ~90,000-100,000 passing through the valley in 20 minutes (the valley is about 2000 ft wide). I know that this number sounds almost unbelievable, but I suspect that the actual total number of birds passing through was more than that if one were to count the flux after sunrise .

Including one bird demonstrating bizarre vocalizations and behavior. While watching a mixed flock at the edge of the woods, I kept hearing a regular "chuck" call coming from the edge of the woods. I could not place the call, so, thinking it might be something rare, I tracked it down. After a long time searching, I found the culprit: a robin, sitting low in a buckthorn, occasionally eating berries but mostly sitting motionless and calling regularly (every few seconds). The bird did not seem totally healthy; every time it called, its body would flinch/convulse (almost like it was hiccupping or sneezing). In addition, its feathers were very puffed out, the bird seemed to be breathing heavily, and whenever it moved it did so slowly and clumsily. Sick? Drunk from fermented berries? Bizarre!

Observation Details: While sitting at the corner of the deck, I heard a high call from behind me. Getting up and walking to the other end of the carport deck while setting up the iPhone to capture the call, I was surprised to see mama robin perched on the railing at the top of the stairs only 3 meters away. I spoke to her as she continued her high downslur calls for 2.5 minutes. She has a nest with two or three nestlings on the beam under the deck about 3 meters from her position. Perhaps she was calling her mate to help with feeding the young. She certainly was not concerned about me, probably because she was used to me talking to her on the first nest she had under the eave which was raided by a raven. Suspect her second nest in the forest had the same result after hearing a commotion there prior to her decision to use the carport beam. Each evening I go out to measure hummingbird syrup consumption and say hello to mama and always say “bye-bye” as I enter the house and any other time I leave the vicinity.

Observation Details: Turdus migratorius migratorius. Resident bird; gray mantle, finely streaked throat. the throat is hard to tell - it often sits facing away from me. However, by looking at the amount of white and the general width of the streaks, it’s possible to see the difference between this and T.m. nigrideus, the other main possible American Robin subspecies here. I use this as the secondary salient characteristic; the back coloration is much less subtle. Wiping bill on telephone wire today.

The throat does seem thicker than I'd expect, but Sibley suggests such features may be clinal, anyway (https://www.sibleyguides.com/bird-info/american-robin/), and I think that the gray mantle is a clincher for T.m.m.. Recorder: Samsung Galaxy J320V phone; Android v7.1.1; App RecForge II
Media notes: I was inside studying when I heard a bird song out my open window that was not as familiar to me. It drove me crazy not knowing what it was. Since the bird continued to sing for about 30-45 minutes, when I got to a stopping point, I decided to go outside and see if I could find it. I was surprised to discover that he was a typical Robin. It was about 400 feet away at the top of a pine tree. I watched him sing through my binoculars while I recorded his song on my phone. It is his whistles, rather than his "cheerily, cheer up, cheer up, cheerily" that I do not recall ever noticing before today. It is a nice, typical, slightly crisp spring morning. This is in a neighborhood.

The single bird called from the top of a tree, then flew to an adjacent tree and called several times from there, then took off (and no other robins were in the air or near it), flew to a high altitude heading south, then seemed to make a deliberate correction and flew southeast.
